_comment: S? Weapons & Armory catalog — real Civil War small arms with loot-game-style rarity tiers (common -> legendary) so procurement feels like Borderlands: spend funds, arm part or all of your army with better guns, raise battlefield firepower/accuracy. Specs grounded in the period record (calibers, effective ranges, rates of fire); `quality` is a 0-100 composite (accuracy + rate-of-fire + range) used by the armory weapon-score. CS-premium arms (csCostMult) cover blockade imports (Enfield, Lorenz) and Northern-made arms the Confederacy could get only by capture or trickle purchase (Colt, Sharps); the repeaters unlock in 1863. Build-injected as GAME_DATA.weapons; consumed by src/55-weapons.js (The Armory tab) and the battle bridge.
provenance: Verified specs (Earl J. Hess, The Rifle Musket in Civil War Combat; American Rifleman; National Park Service weapons references; HISTORICAL-DATA.md weapons tables). Rarity/cost are designer calibration (inferred), tunable.

weapons.name: Model 1842 Musket
weapons.flavor: Buck-and-ball is murderous at fifty yards and useless past a hundred. The war's starting weapon.

weapons.name: Lorenz Rifle
weapons.flavor: Cheap Austrian imports — the third-most-common rifle of the war, serviceable if inconsistent.

weapons.name: Springfield M1861
weapons.flavor: The Union standard — accurate, reliable, single-shot. Over a million made (about 1.5 million with the 1863-64 contract variants). For the South, captured only.

weapons.name: Enfield P1853
weapons.flavor: The British rifle-musket — the most-imported arm of the war, run through the blockade by the hundred thousand.

weapons.name: Richmond Rifle
weapons.flavor: Confederate-made on captured Harpers Ferry machinery — every one a small triumph of Gorgas's bureau.

weapons.name: Colt Revolving Rifle
weapons.flavor: Five fast shots — and the occasional chain-fire that takes your off hand with it.

weapons.name: Sharps Rifle
weapons.flavor: Breech-loading — load lying down and fire three times as fast. Berdan's Sharpshooters' weapon of choice.

weapons.name: Spencer Repeater
weapons.flavor: Seven shots without reloading. At Hoover's Gap and Gettysburg it changed what a battle line could do.

weapons.name: Henry Repeater
weapons.flavor: 'That damned Yankee rifle you load on Sunday and shoot all week.' Sixteen rounds — often bought by soldiers out of their own pockets.

_designNote: Money matters: arming a whole army with Spencers is ruinous; you mix — repeaters for your best brigades, rifle-muskets for the line, muskets for the green. CS pays a scarcity premium on imports and on Northern-made arms (capture/trickle), and cannot buy Springfields (captured only). Artillery (Napoleon, 3-inch Ordnance, Parrott) is a planned second tier.
